reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Source-grounded content: all three prompts, the blank-name → default behavior, and the duplicate-profile error case are all confirmed by src/commands/aws-profile-create/index.ts.

The prose around the examples correctly explains the outcome of each interaction (blank = default, named = usable with --awsProfile), which is exactly what a new user needs before they run the command.
The 'Where credentials are stored' section is a useful orientation for users coming from other tools — it correctly identifies the standard ~/.aws/credentials location without overstating anything.
The Info callout about Console-based access vs. local profiles is a practical pointer that sets expectations for the right audience.
Related commands section is comprehensive and covers all sibling aws-profile commands plus login, which is the correct set.
No forbidden YAML blocks, no StacktapeConfig plain-object style, no marketing language — mandatory style rules are fully respected.
The CliCommandsApiReference component is used correctly with sortedArgs populated from the source.
problems: The '## Important flags' heading is contradictory: it opens with 'This command has no command-specific flags beyond logLevel' and then immediately shows the logLevel flag via CliCommandsApiReference. The heading promises something important, then says there's nothing important. This confuses readers who skim headings.

The Examples section shows the exact same command (stacktape aws-profile:create with no flags) for two different scenarios. Readers who skim code blocks won't know the examples differ — the differentiation is only in the surrounding prose, which scanners skip.

No FAQ section. CLI reference pages benefit from at least a few Q&A pairs covering: what IAM permissions the access key needs, whether existing keys in ~/.aws/credentials from the AWS CLI will conflict, how to switch between profiles per-deploy, and what to do if the command fails because the profile already exists. These are high-intent questions a new user will search.

The section instruction says: 'Lead with one short paragraph... Then a usage example. Then the auto-generated reference.' The 'Important flags' section interleaves an explanatory header between usage and the API reference, which adds noise without value for a command with one optional arg.
mandatoryFixes: Rename '## Important flags' to something that doesn't promise a list of important flags when there is only one optional one. Options: '## Flags' or simply remove the section header and place the CliCommandsApiReference directly after '## Usage', as the section instructions prescribe (usage example → auto-generated reference). Move the 'All prompts are handled interactively' sentence into the Usage section body.
optionalImprovements: Differentiate the two identical-looking bash examples. Since the command has no flags, the difference is in user input at the prompt. Consider showing them as a single annotated example or replacing the second block with a note like 'When prompted for a profile name, enter staging, production, or any label you prefer' rather than repeating an identical code block.

Add a short FAQ section (4–6 pairs) covering: (1) What IAM permissions does the key need? (2) Will profiles I created with the AWS CLI show up here? (3) How do I use a named profile with deploy? (4) What happens if I lose my secret key? (5) Where exactly is the credentials file on Windows vs. Linux/macOS? These are the real questions a first-time user searches.

The first paragraph ('The aws-profile:create command creates a new AWS profile...') is accurate but dense. Consider splitting the single long sentence about 'Other AWS tools — including the AWS CLI — can use these profiles' into a second sentence so AEO extractors get a cleaner snippet.

Consider adding a note that the command requires no AWS account to be previously configured — it is typically the first command a new user runs, before any deploy. This positions the page correctly in the onboarding flow.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Accurately documents all three interactive prompts with correct labels (profile name, Access Key ID, Secret Access Key), confirmed by src/commands/aws-profile-create/index.ts.
Correctly documents the duplicate-profile error behavior: the command fails if the profile exists in either the credentials file OR the config file — matching the two separate checks in the source.
Clean Related commands section covers all four sibling commands (update, delete, list, login) with correct link paths.
Correct use of <CliCommandsApiReference> with the single available flag (logLevel), matching the command's args definition in commands.ts.
The 'Where credentials are stored' section usefully explains AWS tooling compatibility and links to the Console alternative — adding practical context beyond the minimum.
problems: The 'Where credentials are stored' section states credentials 'are written to the standard AWS credentials file at ~/.aws/credentials', but the source only confirms that both ~/.aws/credentials and ~/.aws/config are *checked* for conflicts. The actual write destination is inside `upsertAwsProfile` (src/utils/aws-config), which is not provided. The claim about which specific file receives the write is an unverified assumption.

The 'Important flags' section contains only two sentences saying there are no notable flags, followed immediately by the <CliCommandsApiReference> that already makes this self-evident. The section is near-empty and adds no actionable content.

The Examples section shows two separate code blocks with the exact same command (`stacktape aws-profile:create`) — one for 'default' and one for a named profile. Since the command is fully interactive and the bash lines are identical, the examples are indistinguishable without reading the prose below each block, which undermines their value as quick-reference copy-paste snippets.
optionalImprovements: Soften the credential write-location claim: change 'Credentials are written to the standard AWS credentials file at ~/.aws/credentials' to 'Credentials are stored in the standard AWS credentials location in your home directory' — matching the language in the command's source description and avoiding an unverified claim about the specific file.

Remove or fold the 'Important flags' section: it says nothing the reader can act on, and the <CliCommandsApiReference> immediately below already conveys that only logLevel is available. Either drop the section entirely or merge its one meaningful sentence ('The command is fully interactive') up into the leading paragraph.

Consolidate the two identical bash examples: since both invoke `stacktape aws-profile:create` with no flags, consider one example block followed by a short prose paragraph explaining the two distinct use cases (blank = default, named = specify a profile). Two visually identical blocks with identical copy-button output are more confusing than one.

The `--logLevel error` example at the end of the Examples section has negligible practical value for a fully interactive command — suppressing logs during an interactive prompt is an edge case most readers will never encounter. Consider removing it to keep the examples focused on the primary use cases.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Clear CLI-reference shape: short command purpose, usage, generated argument reference, examples, storage location, and related commands.
Shell snippets follow the one-command-per-block rule and contain no explanatory comments inside copyable command blocks.
The page uses the required `<CliCommandsApiReference command="aws-profile:create" ... />` component and documents the only command-specific flag shown in the source.
The examples are practical for the interactive nature of the command and avoid placeholders.
problems: Several storage-path statements are more specific than the provided source supports. The source says credentials are stored in the default AWS location in the home directory and calls `fsPaths.awsCredentialsFilePath()`, but the provided files do not prove the literal path `~/.aws/credentials`.

The claim that a profile named `default` is used automatically when no `--awsProfile` flag is passed, and the later `--awsProfile staging` example, are not grounded in the provided source files. The source only says `default` is used as the default profile value in every command.
The page says profiles created with Stacktape work with “any AWS tooling on the same machine.” The source supports use by “other AWS tools,” but “any AWS tooling” is broader than the evidence.

The Info callout describes Console-based AWS access as using IAM roles rather than local credential files. That may be true elsewhere, but it is not grounded in the provided source files for this page.

The command has no required flags, so the usage example is valid, but the page could be more explicit that `aws-profile:create` cannot be run non-interactively from flags according to the shown command definition and implementation.
optionalImprovements: Narrow `~/.aws/credentials` wording to “the default AWS credentials location in your home directory” unless the page source set includes `fsPaths.awsCredentialsFilePath()` implementation proving the exact path.

Replace `--awsProfile` claims with source-grounded wording such as “Leaving the profile prompt blank saves the profile as `default`, which Stacktape describes as the default profile value in every command.”
Remove or source the Console IAM-role callout; if kept, add console-app or Console docs source coverage for that statement.
Change “any AWS tooling” to “other AWS tools” to match the command definition exactly.
Consider renaming `Important flags` to `Flags` for a compact CLI-reference heading, since only `logLevel` is listed.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly grounded in the provided CLI command definition and runtime implementation. I found one narrow unsupported precision issue around the exact credentials path; the rest of the command behavior, prompts, duplicate-profile checks, flag table, and related-command links align with the source files and navigation index.

issues.statement: “then stores them in the standard AWS credentials file (`~/.aws/credentials`)” and “Credentials are written to the standard AWS credentials file at `~/.aws/credentials`. This is the same location used by the AWS CLI and AWS SDKs.”
issues.reason: The provided source confirms Stacktape stores credentials in the default AWS location in the user's home directory and reads the credentials/config paths through `fsPaths`, but the supplied files do not show the literal resolved path `~/.aws/credentials` or the exact write behavior of `upsertAwsProfile`. The wording is likely correct AWS background, but the exact Stacktape-specific path is not directly visible in the provided source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to the sourced phrasing: “Credentials are stored in the default AWS credentials location in your home directory.” If keeping `~/.aws/credentials`, phrase it as AWS convention rather than a verified Stacktape implementation detail.

positiveFindings: The draft correctly documents that `aws-profile:create` is interactive and prompts for profile name, AWS Access Key ID, and AWS Secret Access Key.
The duplicate-profile behavior is grounded in `src/commands/aws-profile-create/index.ts`, including checks against both the credentials file and config file.
The `logLevel` flag table matches the command definition: `aws-profile:create` has no command-specific arguments beyond optional `logLevel`.
All internal documentation links in the draft point to routes present in the provided navigation index.
The page uses one command per shell block and does not include forbidden Stacktape configuration examples.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the provided command definition and implementation. I found one unsupported precision issue around the credentials file path because the provided source confirms the default AWS location/home directory and credentials-file helper, but does not expose the literal `~/.aws/credentials` path.

issues.statement: Credentials are written to the standard AWS credentials file at `~/.aws/credentials`.
issues.reason: The provided sources say credentials are stored in the default AWS location in the user's home directory and show the command reading `fsPaths.awsCredentialsFilePath()`, but the literal path `~/.aws/credentials` is not present in the provided source files. The claim is likely true AWS convention, but it is more precise than the supplied source proves.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to: "Credentials are written to Stacktape's AWS credentials file path, which the CLI describes as the default AWS location in your home directory." If keeping `~/.aws/credentials`, add the source file that defines `fsPaths.awsCredentialsFilePath()` to the page sources.

positiveFindings: The prompt flow is accurately described: profile name, AWS access key ID, and AWS secret access key are all present in `src/commands/aws-profile-create/index.ts`.
The default profile behavior is grounded by the prompt description: leaving the profile name blank uses `default`.
The duplicate-profile failure behavior is grounded by explicit checks against both the credentials file and config file.

The `<CliCommandsApiReference command="aws-profile:create" ... />` command name matches the documented command, and the only command-specific argument shown is `logLevel`, matching `src/config/cli/commands.ts`.
All markdown cross-links in the draft point to routes present in the provided documentation navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: API completeness audit found no publication-blocking or medium-severity issues for this CLI reference page. The command has no required args and only exposes the optional `logLevel` argument in the provided command definition, which the draft documents through the generated CLI reference component.
positiveFindings: The draft uses `<CliCommandsApiReference command="aws-profile:create" ... />` instead of hand-authoring the flags table.
The usage examples include the full command invocation; the source defines no required arguments for `aws-profile:create`.
The only exposed argument, `logLevel`, is included with its alias, allowed values, and description.
The draft correctly distinguishes related AWS profile commands: create, update, delete, and list.
